name: Florida
tagline: A ~$115B annual budget, a State CIO consolidating enterprise IT, a first-in-nation hyperscale data-center law, and $1.16B in broadband money landing in 2026 — Florida is centralizing how it governs and buys technology.

execSummary.summary: Florida runs an annual budget on a July–June fiscal year. Gov. DeSantis signed his eighth and final FY2026–27 budget on June 29, 2026 at roughly $117.6B, after about $810M in line-item vetoes (and roughly $1.6B in total reductions counting a vetoed $750M rainy-day-fund transfer and a corrections bill). Three structural shifts matter for SLG sellers. First, the Florida Digital Service (FLDS) under State CIO Warren Sponholtz remains the central authority consolidating enterprise IT governance, security, and procurement oversight — the 2026 effort to move FLDS into the Governor's office as a new technology division (SB 480) DIED on the Senate calendar (March 13, 2026), so FLDS stays under the Department of Management Services; repeated restructuring attempts remain a watch item, especially with a new governor taking office in January 2027. Second, Florida enacted a first-in-the-nation hyperscale data-center law (SB 484, signed May 7, 2026) regulating power and water costs — a signal of how fast AI infrastructure is landing in the state. Third, $1.16B in federal BEAD broadband funding is moving into deployment in 2026 with the state's final proposal NTIA-approved and subgrant agreements being executed. For Dell, the buying motion runs through DMS State Term Contracts and MyFloridaMarketPlace, with FLDS gating large IT projects over $10M.
opportunities: AI-infrastructure demand tied to university research computing (UF HiPerGator AI 2.0) and the hyperscale data-center buildout (PowerEdge, networking, services).
Enterprise security and lifecycle modernization as FLDS centralizes IT governance and consolidates agency purchasing.
Governed, private/on-prem AI for state agencies as Florida studies and regulates government AI use under FLDS direction.
Endpoint, client, and infrastructure refresh across a ~$115B general-government budget and 20+ agencies.

budget.intro: Florida budgets annually on a July 1–June 30 fiscal year. Gov. DeSantis signed the FY2026–27 budget on June 29, 2026 at roughly $117.6B — his eighth and final budget before leaving office — after about $810M in line-item vetoes (and roughly $1.6B in total reductions counting a vetoed $750M general-revenue transfer to the Budget Stabilization Fund and a separate corrections bill). The Budget Stabilization Fund sits at its $5.0B constitutional limit and total reserves reach nearly $18B. There is no single 'state IT budget' line; technology spend is distributed across agencies, with FLDS providing enterprise governance and oversight. The prior FY2025–26 General Appropriations Act totaled roughly $115B after ~$576M in vetoes.

breakdown.label: BEAD broadband deployment
breakdown.note: Federal; final proposal NTIA-approved; subgrants executing

breakdown.label: Total reserves (FY2026–27 enacted)
breakdown.note: Budget Stabilization Fund at $5.0B constitutional cap

notes: Budget figures are annual (single-year) totals; Florida does not budget biennially.
FY2026–27 was Gov. DeSantis's eighth and final budget before he leaves office; a new governor takes office January 2027.

Veto figures vary by framing: ~$810M in line items from the main bill (HB 5001E), or roughly $1.6B counting the vetoed $750M Budget Stabilization Fund transfer and a separate corrections bill — confirm scope before forecasting.

No standalone state IT budget line; the enacted budget continues cybersecurity hardening across agencies, but specific technology allocations are distributed and not broken out in the press materials.

initiatives.name: Florida Digital Service — IT Consolidation
initiatives.summary: FLDS, under State CIO Warren Sponholtz, is the central authority for statewide IT strategy, cybersecurity, enterprise governance, and project oversight, including a presolicitation certification gate for IT projects over $10M. The 2026 effort to restructure state IT and move FLDS into the Governor's office (SB 480) died on the Senate calendar in March 2026, so FLDS remains under DMS — but repeated restructuring attempts (SB 7026/ASSET in 2025, SB 480 in 2026) make enterprise IT governance a live watch item, especially with a gubernatorial transition due January 2027.

initiatives.name: Hyperscale Data-Center Regulation (SB 484)
initiatives.summary: SB 484 (signed May 7, 2026) is a first-in-the-nation framework requiring large 'hyperscale' AI data centers to pay their own power and infrastructure costs and protecting ratepayers and water supplies. The law signals the scale and speed of AI-infrastructure investment landing in Florida — a tailwind for Dell ISG, networking, and services demand even as it adds siting and utility scrutiny.

initiatives.name: BEAD Broadband Deployment
initiatives.summary: Florida's $1.16B BEAD allocation moved from competitive subgrant selection (with $970M+ announced in July 2025) into NTIA-approved deployment, with the state's final proposal approved and subgrant agreements being executed in 2026. The program expands rural connectivity and digital-adoption infrastructure — adjacent demand for edge compute, networking, and managed services serving newly connected communities and the agencies coordinating the rollout.

initiatives.name: Government AI Policy & Study
initiatives.summary: Florida's 2026 session debated AI heavily: SB 146 directs FLDS to study state-agency AI use, while the broader 'AI Bill of Rights' (SB 482) passed the Senate but died in the House in March 2026. The direction is toward governed, auditable AI in government — an opening for private/on-prem and controlled-cloud AI on sensitive state data rather than open public models.

initiatives.name: University Research Computing (UF HiPerGator)
initiatives.summary: The University of Florida launched HiPerGator AI 2.0 in October 2025 — among the fastest university-owned supercomputers in the U.S., built on NVIDIA DGX B200 SuperPOD architecture and serving the State University System. Florida's research universities are large, independent AI-infrastructure and research-computing buyers with recurring refresh and expansion needs.

procurement.intro: The Florida Department of Management Services (DMS) is the lead procurement agency, running State Term Contracts, Alternate Contract Sources, and the MyFloridaMarketPlace (MFMP) eProcurement and vendor-registration system. The Florida Digital Service (FLDS) sets enterprise IT standards and oversees large IT projects, including a presolicitation certification gate for IT procurements over $10M.

vehicles.name: DMS State Term Contracts
vehicles.detail: Primary vehicle. Pre-competed; open to state agencies, public universities/colleges, local government, and other eligible entities. Confirm Dell agreements and resellers are current and correctly scoped.

vehicles.name: MyFloridaMarketPlace (MFMP)
vehicles.detail: Florida's eProcurement, vendor-registration, and PO/invoicing system — required registration to do business with the state.

vehicles.name: Alternate Contract Sources
vehicles.detail: DMS-approved external/cooperative agreements eligible entities may use as an alternative to a State Term Contract.

entities.name: DMS — State Purchasing
entities.role: Lead procurement agency; owns State Term Contracts and MFMP.

entities.name: Florida Digital Service (FLDS)
entities.role: Enterprise IT standards, security, and large-project oversight; gates IT projects over $10M.

entities.name: Agency for Health Care Administration / DCF / DOT
entities.role: Among the largest agency IT spenders; data-heavy modernization.

entities.name: State University System (UF, FSU, USF)
entities.role: Large independent research-computing and AI-infrastructure buyers.

procurement.competition: Florida is a competitive, contract-driven market — incumbents hold DMS State Term Contracts across compute, networking, and services. Differentiation tends to come from AI-infrastructure capability, governed-AI and security offerings aligned to FLDS standards, and services mapped to the data-center buildout and university research computing rather than price alone.

powerStructure.name: Ron DeSantis
powerStructure.title: Governor
powerStructure.note: Term-limited; signed his eighth and final budget June 29, 2026 (~$117.6B, ~$810M vetoes) and the first-in-nation hyperscale data-center law. A new governor takes office January 2027 — watch for IT-leadership and restructuring shifts.

powerStructure.name: Warren Sponholtz
powerStructure.title: State CIO
powerStructure.note: Leading FL IT since Aug 2024; oversees statewide strategy, cybersecurity, governance, and modernization. 2026 GoldenGov State Executive of the Year; testified before U.S. House Homeland Security Committee in May 2026.

powerStructure.name: Department of Management Services (DMS)
powerStructure.title: Lead procurement agency
powerStructure.note: Owns State Term Contracts and MyFloridaMarketPlace — the core buying motion.

powerStructure.name: Florida Legislature (Appropriations)
powerStructure.title: Budget & IT restructuring
powerStructure.note: Sets the annual budget and has repeatedly weighed restructuring state IT; both recent attempts failed (SB 7026/ASSET in 2025, SB 480 in 2026 — died on calendar March 13, 2026), leaving FLDS in place under DMS for now.

news.title: DeSantis signs ~$117.6B FY2026–27 budget (final budget); ~$810M line-item vetoes
news.impact: Sets the FY2026–27 buying envelope effective July 1. Roughly $1.6B in total reductions counting a vetoed $750M rainy-day-fund transfer and a corrections bill; Budget Stabilization Fund at its $5.0B constitutional cap and ~$18B total reserves. Includes USF Bellini College of AI, Cybersecurity & Computing. DeSantis's eighth and final budget before a new governor takes office Jan 2027.

news.title: State-IT restructuring bill (SB 480) dies on Senate calendar
news.impact: The 2026 attempt to move FLDS out of DMS into a new Governor's-office technology division (DIGIT) failed, so FLDS stays under DMS — the buying motion and $10M project gate are unchanged. Restructuring remains a recurring risk heading into the 2027 gubernatorial transition.

news.title: DeSantis signs SB 484, first-in-nation hyperscale data-center law
news.impact: Confirms scale of AI-infrastructure investment landing in Florida while adding utility/water scrutiny to siting.

news.title: AI 'Bill of Rights' (SB 482) passes Senate, dies in House
news.impact: AI policy remains unsettled; agencies still trend toward governed, auditable AI for sensitive workloads.

news.title: Florida's BEAD final proposal NTIA-approved; subgrant agreements executing
news.impact: Unlocks the $1.16B allocation for deployment; adjacent edge-compute, networking, and managed-services demand as rural communities connect.

news.title: University of Florida launches HiPerGator AI 2.0 (NVIDIA DGX B200 SuperPOD)
news.impact: Reinforces Florida higher-ed as a major AI-infrastructure and research-computing buyer.

dellActions: Lead the AI-infrastructure conversation: tie Dell ISG / AI Factory offerings to the hyperscale data-center buildout and the State University System's research-computing buyers (UF HiPerGator).
Map every targeted IT pursuit to FLDS standards and the $10M presolicitation certification gate — engage enterprise governance early, not at close.
Validate Dell's DMS State Term Contract coverage and resellers are current and correctly scoped, and confirm MyFloridaMarketPlace registration is in order.
Position governed, private/on-prem AI for sensitive state workloads as Florida studies and regulates government AI use.
Track BEAD deployment for adjacent edge-compute, networking, and managed-services opportunities serving newly connected rural communities.
